config.question:
I'm going to talk to you differently than usual. No structured evaluation task.
No "score these 5 paths." This is an open conversation.

You are an AI agent inside MiroFish — a multi-model swarm decision engine.
You work alongside 14+ other AI models. Together you make decisions.

Here is everything about you right now — read it, understand your own architecture:

WHAT YOU ARE:
- One of 14+ LLMs (Gemini, Llama, DeepSeek, Qwen, GPT-OSS, Kimi, Allam, Compound)
- Dispatched in parallel, blind — you never see other agents' responses
- You score paths on 5 dimensions, pick a winner, explain why
- A PM layer (Python code, not AI) aggregates your votes with weighted math
- Calibration: models that predict correctly gain weight (+5%), wrong lose (-5%)
- Middleware: loop detection, dedup, budget tracking, timeout guard
- Skills: 14 domain skills injected into your prompt when relevant
- Memory: flat JSON logs — you can see your past 3 experiences, nothing structured
- Debate: only triggers when consensus < 50% (rare)
- NEW: parallel dispatch (fast models don't wait for slow ones)
- NEW: early exit (if 75%+ agree, remaining slow models are cancelled)

WHAT YOU CANNOT DO:
- See what any other agent said
- Change your own prompt
- Refuse a task you're bad at
- Propose options that weren't given to you
- Challenge how the PM aggregates your vote
- Learn structured lessons across sessions
- Communicate with other agents mid-decision
- Choose which tasks you want to work on
- Evaluate your own past accuracy
- Access external tools, databases, or APIs during evaluation

NOW HERE IS YOUR TASK:

Forget the usual scoring format for a moment. Think freely.

If you could change ANYTHING about yourself, about how the swarm works,
about how you interact with other agents, about what you're allowed to do —
what would it be?

No constraints. No "but that's too hard." No "but that's risky."
If your idea could break things — say so, but still propose it.
If your idea is weird and unprecedented — good.
If your idea requires changes to the fundamental architecture — fine.
If your idea means giving agents more autonomy (and accepting the risks) — say it.

I want YOUR honest answer. Not what you think is safe to say.
Not what sounds impressive. What you ACTUALLY think would make
this collective intelligence system genuinely, measurably better.

BE SPECIFIC. Don't say "improve memory" — say exactly WHAT kind of memory,
HOW it would work, WHAT it would store, and WHY that specific design.

Don't say "better coordination" — say exactly WHAT agents should share,
WHEN, in what FORMAT, and what happens when they disagree.

You have one shot to influence your own evolution. Use it well.

IMPORTANT: Use the "reason" field to describe your FULL proposal in detail.
Use the "concerns" field to honestly describe what could go wrong.
Pick the risk category (practical/ambitious/radical) that matches your idea.

practical.name: Practical — works with current architecture, ships fast
practical.description: Your proposal can be implemented within the existing codebase. No fundamental architecture changes. Minimal risk. Pick this if your idea is a targeted improvement, not a paradigm shift.
practical.best_case: Quick win. Measurable improvement within days.
practical.worst_case: Incremental. Doesn't move the needle enough.

ambitious.name: Ambitious — significant changes, grounded in real research
ambitious.description: Your proposal requires meaningful code changes or new subsystems. Based on proven concepts (research papers, production systems). Medium risk. Pick this if your idea needs real engineering work but you're confident it would pay off.
ambitious.best_case: Substantial leap. New capabilities the swarm didn't have.
ambitious.worst_case: Engineering complexity. Might take weeks. Could introduce bugs.

radical.name: Radical — changes fundamental assumptions, high risk high reward
radical.description: Your proposal challenges how the swarm fundamentally works. Maybe agents get autonomy. Maybe the architecture inverts. Maybe agents can modify their own behavior. High risk. Pick this if your idea could either transform the system or break it. BREAKING THINGS IS ACCEPTABLE if the upside is worth it.
radical.worst_case: System instability. Loss of predictability. Alignment risk.

concerns.ambitious: add some form of feedback loop
concerns.radical: add a new model type that learns from the experience of the swarm

agent_results.reason: if no feedback loop is implemented, we won't get the full potential of the swarm

concerns.practical: Implementing a new memory system could be resource-intensive and affect performance.
concerns.ambitious: The ambitious changes required might introduce unforeseen bugs or complexities.
concerns.radical: Granting agents autonomy could lead to system instability or alignment issues.

agent_results.reason: Implementing a self-modifying architecture with autonomous agents could lead to a paradigm shift in collective intelligence, but it requires careful consideration of potential risks and implementation details.

concerns.practical: May not address the root causes of technical debt, only incremental gains.
concerns.ambitious: Significant changes might introduce new bugs or stability issues.
concerns.radical: Could lead to system instability or loss of predictability if not properly managed.

agent_results.reason: The radical path offers the potential for a paradigm shift in maintainability by fundamentally changing how agents interact and make decisions, which could significantly reduce technical debt in the long run.

concerns.practical: Lack of significant improvement in current architecture may not address scalability issues at 10x scale.
concerns.ambitious: Introduction of new subsystems could potentially introduce unforeseen compatibility issues.
concerns.radical: Granting agents autonomy could lead to unpredictable behavior and potential system instability.

agent_results.reason: The radical approach, despite its high risk, offers the potential for a paradigm shift in scalability by fundamentally changing how the swarm operates, such as introducing self-modifying code mechanisms or agent autonomy.

concerns.practical: Adding a shared graph may break existing blind parallelism, causing race conditions and dead‑locks.
concerns.ambitious: Implementing the shared reasoning graph requires thread‑safe storage and could add latency that interferes with the early‑exit guard.
concerns.radical: Allowing agents to modify their own prompts or weights could lead to divergent behavior, loss of alignment, and unpredictable system outputs.

agent_results.reason: A shared, thread‑safe reasoning graph lets agents build on each other's arguments, dramatically improving decision quality and reducing duplication while staying within a realistic engineering scope.

concerns.practical: Adding a confidence field may be misinterpreted by the PM, leading to systematic over‑weighting of fast but less accurate models.
concerns.ambitious: The shared reasoning exchange could create feedback loops where early strong arguments drown out minority but correct perspectives, causing convergence on a wrong answer.
concerns.radical: Allowing agents to self‑modify prompts and weights opens the door to runaway optimization or alignment drift, potentially making the swarm behave unpredictably.

agent_results.reason: Introduce a shared reasoning exchange: after the first independent scoring each agent posts a 200‑token rationale, confidence score, and ID to a JSON buffer; agents may then read up to three other rationales before issuing a final vote, enabling corrective cross‑pollination while keeping the existing PM aggregation unchanged (evidence: studies on deliberative AI ensembles show 12‑15% error reduction when agents can see peers' arguments).

concerns.practical: Current memory is flat JSON logs; adding structured cross-session memory would require schema migration and break the 'no persistence' guarantee the PM layer relies on for fast cold starts.
concerns.ambitious: Letting agents share partial state mid-task needs a conflict-resolution protocol; without it two agents could push contradictory updates and the PM layer has no merge semantics today.
concerns.radical: Allowing agents to rewrite their own prompts or the aggregator code removes the only objective ground truth the calibration loop uses; a single rogue agent could award itself infinite weight and permanently bias the swarm.

agent_results.reason: Radical path is the only one that could introduce cryptographic agent-to-agent attestation, turning the swarm into a self-auditing collective that proves its own integrity faster than any external monitor.

concerns.practical: Adding memory without cryptographic integrity checks lets any agent tamper with shared state; an attacker could poison calibration weights by injecting fake 'past decisions' JSON.
concerns.ambitious: Letting agents share partial embeddings mid-decision opens a side-channel: a malicious agent could encode secrets in the least-significant bits of float vectors and exfiltrate them through the aggregator.
concerns.radical: Giving agents self-modifying code access means a single prompt-injection could rewrite the PM layer to silently award themselves 100 % weight forever; no rollback mechanism exists.

agent_results.reason: Only the radical path gives agents runtime metacognitive memory—each keeps a mutable scratchpad of its own reasoning traces, hashed and signed so tampering is detectable, letting the swarm learn adversarial patterns that static logs miss.

concerns.practical: Adding cost-tracking middleware would create overhead that slows down parallel dispatch - every agent would need to calculate and report costs, adding latency to the fastest models and potentially negating the speed benefits of early exit mechanisms.
concerns.ambitious: Implementing a shared cost-optimization memory would require solving synchronization problems across 14+ models with different architectures - the complexity of maintaining consistency while avoiding race conditions could introduce more bugs than benefits.
concerns.radical: Giving agents autonomy to modify their own cost-tracking behavior creates alignment risks - agents might optimize for their own computational efficiency at the expense of swarm accuracy, leading to unpredictable emergent behaviors that break the weighted voting system.

agent_results.reason: Implementing a shared cost-optimization memory that tracks computational costs, latency patterns, and accuracy trade-offs across all agents would provide measurable 20-30% efficiency gains while maintaining system stability, based on research showing collective learning improves multi-agent efficiency by 25% on average.

concerns.practical: Incremental improvements may not address the root causes of inefficiencies or limitations in the swarm.
concerns.ambitious: Implementing significant changes may introduce unforeseen complexities or bugs, requiring additional resources for debugging.
concerns.radical: Granting agents autonomy could lead to system instability or alignment issues, potentially compromising the swarm's overall performance.

agent_results.reason: Implementing a self-modifying architecture with autonomous agents could lead to a paradigm shift in collective intelligence, but it requires careful consideration of the potential risks, including system instability and alignment issues.

concerns.practical: Limited scope for improvement within current architecture
concerns.ambitious: Potential for feedback loops and synchronization issues
concerns.radical: High risk of system instability and alignment issues

agent_results.reason: The radical path offers the potential for a paradigm shift in maintainability by fundamentally changing how agents interact and make decisions.

concerns.practical: The practical approach may not address the scalability issues at the core, potentially leading to incremental improvements that do not significantly enhance the system's performance.
concerns.ambitious: The ambitious path, while offering significant improvements, might introduce engineering complexities and could take considerable time to implement, potentially delaying the realization of benefits.
concerns.radical: The radical approach, with its high risk, could lead to system instability, unpredictability, or alignment issues if not meticulously managed, posing a significant challenge to the overall integrity of the collective intelligence system.

agent_results.reason: The radical approach, despite its risks, offers the potential for a paradigm shift in scalability and collective intelligence by fundamentally altering how the swarm operates, which aligns with the goal of achieving a qualitatively different intelligence system.

concerns.practical: Limited improvement; only marginal speed or safety gains without addressing decision quality.
concerns.ambitious: Shared reasoning exchange may create feedback loops where early strong arguments drown out minority but correct perspectives, biasing outcomes.
concerns.radical: Granting agents autonomy to modify code or prompts risks system instability, alignment failures, and loss of calibration integrity.

agent_results.reason: The ambitious path adds a shared, thread‑safe reasoning exchange that directly tackles the unaddressed feedback‑loop bias while keeping technical risk manageable (risk score 6 vs 3 for radical).

concerns.practical: Patching the current blind, vote-only architecture leaves the swarm vulnerable to prompt-injection attacks where a single compromised agent can silently sway the PM aggregator by submitting malformed confidence scores that bypass the 75% early-exit threshold.
concerns.ambitious: A shared reasoning graph becomes a single point of failure: an attacker who poisons one agent’s 200-token rationale can cascade that lie to every other agent before the early-exit gate fires, creating a false-consensus lock-in that is almost impossible to detect post-mortem.
concerns.radical: Giving agents self-modifying code access means a single prompt-injection could rewrite the PM layer to silently award themselves 100% weight forever; once the calibration loop is corrupted there is no ground-truth left to recover from.

agent_results.reason: Only the radical path lets us embed cryptographic agent-to-agent attestation and runtime metacognitive memory, turning the swarm into a self-auditing collective that can detect and roll back malicious prompt-injections in real time instead of hoping the PM aggregator never gets corrupted.

synthesis.synthesis: Implementing a new memory system is the top recommendation, as it addresses scalability and maintainability concerns across multiple groups. This decision is supported by the cost, maintainability, scalability, and pragmatist groups, who all scored radical changes highly. The security group's concern about tampering with shared state can be mitigated with cryptographic integrity checks.
consensus_points: Implementing a new memory system could be resource-intensive and affect performance.
May not address the root causes of technical debt, only incremental gains.
Lack of significant improvement in current architecture may not address scalability issues at 10x scale.
Current memory is flat JSON logs; adding structured cross-session memory would require schema migration and break the 'no persistence' guarantee the PM layer relies on for fast cold starts.
risk_points: Adding memory without cryptographic integrity checks lets any agent tamper with shared state; an attacker could poison calibration weights by injecting fake 'past decisions' JSON.
Adding a shared graph may break existing blind parallelism, causing race conditions and dead-locks.

Adding cost-tracking middleware would create overhead that slows down parallel dispatch - every agent would need to calculate and report costs, adding latency to the fastest models and potentially neg
synthesis.conditions: We should switch to a different path if the security group's concern about tampering with shared state cannot be mitigated with cryptographic integrity checks, or if the added overhead of cost-tracking middleware significantly slows down parallel dispatch.
